title: Entanglement Enhanced Sensing with Qubits affected by non-Markovian Dephasing
authors: Noah Kaufmann, Kasper Hede Nielsen, Anders Søndberg Sørensen
abstract: Entanglement has been proposed as a means to improve the sensitivity of sensing weak signals. While the degree of this quantum advantage is well understood in noiseless settings, the situation is more complex under realistic conditions, where the system is subject to decoherence. In this case, the enhancement depends on the specific noise characteristics. Previous treatments of colored noise typically assume that the noise is uncorrelated between successive experiments. Here, we consider the scenario in which the noise exhibits correlations across multiple shots. We derive a simple fundamental limit to the sensitivity based on the fact that the sensitivity cannot be better than the signal-to-noise ratio seen by the probe. Focusing on Ramsey spectroscopy with probes affected by pure classical dephasing, we show that, for suitable spatial and temporal noise correlations, entangled probes achieve a better scaling of the sensitivity with the number of probes than separable states. This demonstrates that entanglement can provide a substantial improvement for Ramsey spectroscopy subject to correlated noise.
summary: This paper explores how entanglement can improve the precision of frequency estimation in the presence of complex, non-Markovian noise. It demonstrates that the ability to surpass the Standard Quantum Limit depends critically on whether the noise spectrum has a non-zero component at zero frequency. For specific types of noise, such as Ohmic or Linear noise, the authors show that spin-squeezed states can achieve significantly better scaling of sensing uncertainty with the number of probes than separable states. This provides important theoretical insights for developing quantum sensors that are resilient to correlated environmental noise.
main_problem: Investigating whether entanglement, specifically via spin-squeezed states, can enhance the sensitivity of frequency estimation in Ramsey spectroscopy when qubits are subject to non-Markovian, spatially and temporally correlated dephasing noise.
main_result: The study finds that while entanglement provides no scaling advantage for noise with a non-zero zero-frequency component (like White or Gaussian noise), it enables scaling beyond the Standard Quantum Limit for noise with a vanishing zero-frequency component (like Linear or Ohmic noise), achieving uncertainties as low as N^{-3/4}.
method: The authors use an analytical framework involving Fourier decomposition of the effective Hamiltonian, the Wiener-Khinchin theorem, and optimization of interrogation time and squeezing parameters to derive scaling laws for various noise spectra.
model_or_system: A collection of N two-level systems (qubits) undergoing Ramsey spectroscopy, subject to a classical, pure dephasing noise model that is stationary, translationally invariant, and non-Markovian.
key_observables: Estimation uncertainty (variance) of the frequency b, metrological gain (r), and dephasing rate gamma(tau).
important_parameters: Number of probes (N), total interrogation time (T), squeezing parameter (kappa), and noise power spectra (White, Gaussian, Linear, Ohmic).
main_assumptions: The noise is treated as a classical stochastic process (valid for high-temperature bosonic baths), the dephasing is pure (no X or Y components), and the analysis assumes the long-time and large-N limits.
figures_summary: Figure 4 shows metrological gain as a function of N for various noise combinations; Figure 8 displays scaled uncertainty, metrological gain, and optimal interrogation time for GHZ states across different noise spectra.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of sensing under correlated noise, defines the Hamiltonian and noise models, derives a fundamental sensitivity limit based on SNR, analyzes specific scaling laws for different noise spectra (White, Gaussian, Linear, Ohmic), compares different entangled states (OAT, TAT, GHZ), and concludes with optimization results.
why_interesting: This paper is highly relevant for researchers in open quantum systems and quantum metrology because it identifies specific noise-correlation regimes where entanglement-enhanced sensing can bypass the Standard Quantum Limit, providing a roadmap for designing robust quantum sensors.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. (a) Circuit representation of Ramsey spectroscopy using spin-squeezed states. Applying squeezing to the N-qubit ground state reduces J2 y and, to a lesser degree, ⟨Jz⟩. Next, a π/2 pulse (Rπ/2) rotating the collective spin around the Jy-axis is applied. The state subsequently freely evolves for a time τ, during which it accumulates a relative phase proportional to the energy splitting. This phase is finally estimated by applying another π/2 pulse and a measurement in the Jz-basis. The total procedure is repeated T/τ times. Figure 5. Dependence of the expectation values most relevant to the estimation uncertainty on the squeezing parameter κ for N = 1000 (solid lines) and N = 1001 (dashed lines) qubits. The different behavior as κ →0 occurs because states with an even number of qubits approach |ψκ→0⟩= |0⟩y, while states with an odd number of qubits converge to |ψκ→0⟩∝|1/2⟩y − |−1/2⟩y.

Figure 7. Estimation uncertainty of Ramsey spectroscopy with a single particle as a function of the total time T for different noise spectra. (a) Plot of the considered white, Gaus- sian, linear, and Ohmic noise spectra S(ω), with σ/A = 1/103. (b) Normalized dephasing rate γ(T)/T as a function of the total time T. For white noise γ(T) ∝T, Gaussian noise with temporal correlations leads to γ(T) ∝T 2, the anti- correlations of linear noise result in noise cancellations, and for Ohmic noise, the noise is first correlated and for longer times anti-correlated, resulting in noise γ(t) peaking around σT = 1.
